title: Circe vs The Song of Achilles: Head to Head Comparison
metaDescription: Compare Circe vs The Song of Achilles. Madeline Miller's two Greek mythology masterpieces - which retelling should you read first?

book1.title: Circe
book1.author: Madeline Miller
book1.description: Circe, daughter of the sun god Helios, discovers her gift for witchcraft and is banished to the island of Aiaia. Over centuries, she hones her craft, encounters famous heroes like Odysseus, and struggles to find her place between gods and mortals. Miller transforms Circe from a one-dimensional witch in Homer's Odyssey into a fully realized character exploring themes of power, isolation, motherhood, and self-determination. This feminist retelling gives voice to a character previously defined by men.

book2.title: The Song of Achilles
book2.author: Madeline Miller
book2.description: Patroclus, an exiled prince, becomes the companion and lover of Achilles, Greece's greatest warrior. Told from Patroclus' perspective, the novel follows their relationship from boyhood through the Trojan War. Miller reimagines the Iliad as an epic love story, exploring the bond between these two men and the tragedy that unfolds. The book won the Orange Prize and became a modern classic, introducing Greek mythology to a new generation through intimate, emotional storytelling.

book1Strengths: Circe's voice is powerful and distinctive
Less emotionally devastating than Song of Achilles
book1Weaknesses: The immortal timespan can feel distant
Less emotionally intense than Song of Achilles
Some readers prefer the focused love story
book2Strengths: Higher rating (4.5) shows stronger emotional impact
Won the Orange Prize for Fiction
The love story is utterly devastating
Patroclus' voice is tender and beautiful
Miller's debut that launched her career
book2Weaknesses: The tragic ending can be hard to bear
LGBTQ+ love story may not appeal to all
You know the tragic ending is coming

book1: But in a solitary life, there are rare moments when another soul dips near yours, as stars once a year brush the earth. Such a constellation was he to me.
When I was born, the word for what I was did not exist.
It is a common saying that women are delicate creatures, flowers, eggs, anything that may be crushed in a moment's carelessness. If I had ever believed it, I no longer did.
I thought: I cannot bear this world a moment longer. Then, child, make another.
Not every god need be the same.
Humbling women seems to me a chief pastime of poets. As if there can be no story unless we crawl and weep.
book2: I could recognize him by touch alone, by smell; I would know him blind, by the way his breaths came and his feet struck the earth. I would know him in death, at the end of the world.
He is half of my soul, as the poets say.
We were like gods at the dawning of the world, and our joy was so bright we could see nothing else but the other.
Name one hero who was happy.
I will never leave him. It will be this, always, for as long as he will let me.
In the darkness, two shadows, reaching through the hopeless, heavy dusk. Their hands meet, and light spills in a flood like a hundred golden urns pouring out of the sun.

book1: You want a feminist retelling of mythology
You love character growth over centuries
You want multiple storylines and encounters
You're interested in witchcraft and magic
You want something less emotionally devastating
book2: You want one of the most beautiful love stories ever written
You're okay with emotionally devastating endings
You prefer focused narrative over epic scope
You want to understand why everyone cries

verdict.summary: The Song of Achilles wins with its higher rating (4.5 vs 4.3) and Orange Prize. It's more emotionally powerful and focused. Circe is more popular (920K vs 875K) and empowering, but less devastating. We think Song of Achilles is the superior emotional experience, while Circe is the more comfortable read.
verdict.recommendation: Read The Song of Achilles if you want to experience one of the most beautiful, heartbreaking love stories in modern literature. Yes, you'll cry. Yes, you know the tragic ending. But the journey is worth it. Miller's debut is her masterpiece. Read Circe if you want a longer, more hopeful journey with a powerful female protagonist. It's feminist, empowering, and won't destroy you emotionally. If you only read one, choose Song of Achilles for emotional impact or Circe for empowerment. But honestly, these are both modern classics. Most readers end up reading both. Start with Song of Achilles to understand why Miller became famous, then read Circe for a different but equally beautiful experience. In our view, Song of Achilles is the book that breaks your heart, Circe is the book that puts it back together. Read them in that order for maximum impact.
